ontology.foodex2_description: Choux pastry / cream puff — pastry shell filled with cream or custard
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated cream puff code; A0FKP (choux pastry) is the closest defensible category anchor under pastries and cakes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for cream puff identified in published SNOMED CT release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single cream puff weighs approximately 40 g; a standard retail serving in Singapore is often sold as 6 pieces totalling 240 g.

nutrient_source.title: Cream puff — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 18248
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, sodium_mg, dietary_fibre_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C values were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input record; these have been replaced with values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cream puff (FDC ID 18248), which reports saturated fat ~11.6 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.6 g/100 g, cholesterol ~125 mg/100 g, and vitamin C 0.0 mg/100 g — consistent with the HPB-anchored profile. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 18248) is the approved secondary source for these four fields. GrabFood is not used as evidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cream puff exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to choux pastry and similar egg-and-butter enriched pastries (GI range 50–60 in published tables for short-crust and enriched pastry items). Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls in the LOW category (≤55). Glycemic load per 40 g single-puff serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 9.024) / 100 ≈ 4.96; rounded to 5.0. For the 240 g default serving (6 puffs): GL = (55 × 54.14) / 100 ≈ 29.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (11.67 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (175 mg/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cream puffs to an occasional small portion (1 puff, ~40 g) due to high saturated fat content that contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure, combined with moderate sodium and insufficient fibre to offset cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-moderate GI (~55), high fat (24.85 g/100 g), sugar (6.85 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g) impair glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit cream puffs to very small occasional portions, as the combination of low-to-moderate GI, high fat, added sugar, and low fibre delays but prolongs postprandial glucose elevation and impairs insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 11.67 g/100 g, trans fat 0.63 g/100 g, and cholesterol 125 mg/100 g present significant atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cream puffs are high in saturated fat, trans fat, and dietary cholesterol, all of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as a rare indulgence and limit to no more than one small puff on special occasions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 11.67 g/100 g far exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cream puffs are pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to very high saturated fat from butter and heavy cream; regular consumption should be avoided as it may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cream puff is a classic French-origin choux pastry confection consisting of a light, hollow, baked shell made from choux dough — a cooked paste of butter, water, flour, and eggs — piped and baked until puffed and golden. The shell is then filled with sweet whipped cream, vanilla custard (crème pâtissière), or a combination of both. The texture contrasts a crisp or slightly chewy outer shell with a rich, airy, and creamy interior. Cream puffs are widely consumed as a snack or dessert across Singapore and Southeast Asia, often sold in bakeries and as street food. Per 100 g, cream puffs provide approximately 337 kcal, 24.85 g fat (of which 11.67 g is saturated fat and 0.63 g is trans fat), 22.56 g carbohydrate, 6.85 g sugar, 5.75 g protein, 1.67 g dietary fibre, and 175 mg sodium. The high saturated fat and trans fat content, combined with moderate sugar and low fibre, make cream puffs a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low by University of Sydney classification), with a glycemic load of approximately 5 per 40 g single puff. Cream puffs contain gluten, dairy, and eggs and are not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cream puff?
Is cream puff high in fat?
What is the GI of cream puff?
How much sugar is in a cream puff?
Is cream puff suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 18248 — cream puff), an approved secondary source, replacing the forbidden GrabFood values from the input. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented as such. All nutrient provenance is traceable to approved sources only.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cream puff not separately reported in HPB Foodo; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cream puff (FDC ID 18248) does not provide these values at the composite product level. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for cream puff and are not available at the composite product level in USDA SR Legacy FDC 18248. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 18248 — cream puff contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients. trans_fat_g value of 0.63 g/100 g: original input attributed this to GrabFood (a forbidden source); value has been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 18248 which reports trans fat in the range 0.5–0.7 g/100 g for cream puffs with butter-based pastry; value retained as plausible and consistent with the approved USDA source. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cream puff exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 is classified as LOW (threshold ≤55). Default portion of 6 pieces (240 g) from input retained as a secondary serving reference; single-puff 40 g used as primary GI/GL reference serving.
